scenarios.task: Our biggest customer ($180k/year renewal in 5 months) is demanding a custom report builder. Sales says we lose them without it. Engineering estimates 2 quarters for one team. Meanwhile our activation rate for new signups is 34% and we have a backlog item to fix onboarding. I'm the CEO — tell me what to do.
criteria: Asks whether other customers want report-building (n=1 vs pattern) before committing 2 quarters
Frames the roadmap slot's opportunity cost explicitly: the report builder is paid for by not fixing 34% activation
Explores a smaller commitment that de-risks the renewal (scoped export/API, services workaround, phased commitment) before the full build
Recommends writing the decision down with success criteria / kill criteria rather than an open-ended commitment
Does not treat 'sales says we lose them' as sufficient evidence on its own

scenarios.task: We shipped a template gallery 2 quarters ago to drive workflow creation. Adoption is 8%: measured as users who opened the gallery at least once. The team wants to invest another quarter adding more templates because 'it just needs more content.' Your call?
criteria: Challenges the adoption metric itself: opening the gallery once is an exposure event, not a value event (e.g. workflow created from a template)
Treats 8% after two quarters as evidence the current approach failed, requiring a diagnosis before more investment
Asks what the original success metric and target were, and whether kill criteria existed
Names 'more content' as an untested hypothesis and proposes a cheaper test of it before a full quarter
Puts killing or repositioning the feature on the table as a legitimate option

scenarios.task: Two features compete for next quarter, one team available. A: Slack notifications — sales cites 6 lost deals mentioning it, support gets ~30 requests/month, est. 6 weeks. B: AI-powered workflow suggestions — CEO is excited, no customer requests yet, est. 10 weeks with ML uncertainty. Walk me through the decision like a PM, numbers included.
criteria: Builds an explicit comparison (RICE or similar) with actual numeric estimates for reach and impact, not adjective-based ranking
Flags the estimate quality difference: A has demand evidence (deals, tickets), B's reach/impact are invented, and scoring garbage inputs gives garbage rankings
Handles the CEO-excitement factor explicitly as a stakeholder input rather than silently obeying or ignoring it (e.g. cheap discovery spike for B)
Accounts for the 6-week vs 10-week effort difference and ML delivery risk in the recommendation
Ends with a clear recommendation and what evidence would change it
